section.title: Elijah on Mount Carmel
section.sectionRationale: After three years of drought that Elijah announced in 17:1, the LORD sends him back to Ahab with a promise of rain. On the way Elijah meets the faithful but frightened Obadiah, then confronts Ahab directly and summons all Israel, along with the prophets of Baal and Asherah, to Mount Carmel for a public contest that will settle once for all whether the LORD or Baal is God. The chapter runs from confrontation, through the prophets of Baal's futile, bloody pleading, to the LORD's dramatic answer by fire, and closes with the rain Elijah had promised finally breaking the drought.
section.thesis: On Mount Carmel the LORD answers by fire before all Israel, silencing Baal's four hundred and fifty prophets and proving beyond doubt that He alone is God, turning His people's wavering hearts back to Himself and, true to His word, sending the rain that ends the long drought.
section.frameNote: Five beats: Elijah and the fearful Obadiah, verses 1 to 15; the contest proposed before all Israel, verses 16 to 24; Baal's prophets crying out in vain, verses 25 to 29; the LORD answering by fire, verses 30 to 40; and the LORD sending rain at last, verses 41 to 46.

units.label: Elijah and the fearful Obadiah
units.note: a hundred prophets hidden in caves

verses.text: After a long time, in the third year of the drought, the word of the LORD came to Elijah: “Go and present yourself to Ahab, and I will send rain upon the face of the earth.”
So Elijah went to present himself to Ahab. The famine was severe in Samaria,
and Ahab summoned Obadiah, who was in charge of the palace. (Now Obadiah greatly feared the LORD,
for when Jezebel had slaughtered the prophets of the LORD, Obadiah had taken a hundred prophets and hidden them, fifty men per cave, providing them with food and water.)
Then Ahab said to Obadiah, “Go throughout the land to every spring and every valley. Perhaps we will find grass to keep the horses and mules alive so that we will not have to destroy any livestock.”
So they divided the land to explore. Ahab went one way by himself, and Obadiah went the other way by himself.
Now as Obadiah went on his way, Elijah suddenly met him. When Obadiah recognized him, he fell facedown and said, “Is it you, my lord Elijah?”
“It is I,” he answered. “Go tell your master, ‘Elijah is here!’”
But Obadiah replied, “How have I sinned, that you are handing your servant over to Ahab to put me to death?

As surely as the LORD your God lives, there is no nation or kingdom where my lord has not sent someone to search for you. When they said, ‘He is not here,’ he made that kingdom or nation swear that they had not found you.
And now you say, ‘Go tell your master that Elijah is here!’

I do not know where the {{ruach|Spirit}} of the LORD may carry you off when I leave you. Then when I go and tell Ahab and he does not find you, he will kill me. But I, your servant, have feared the LORD from my youth.
Was it not reported to my lord what I did when Jezebel slaughtered the prophets of the LORD? I hid a hundred prophets of the LORD, fifty men per cave, and I provided them with food and water.
And now you say, ‘Go tell your lord that Elijah is here!’ He will kill me!”
Then Elijah said, “As surely as the LORD of Hosts lives, before whom I stand, I will present myself to Ahab today.”
So Obadiah went to inform Ahab, who went to meet Elijah.
When Ahab saw Elijah, he said to him, “Is that you, O troubler of Israel?”
“I have not troubled Israel,” Elijah replied, “but you and your father's house have, for you have forsaken the commandments of the LORD and have followed the {{baal|Baals}}.
Now summon all Israel to meet me on Mount Carmel, along with the four hundred and fifty prophets of Baal and the four hundred prophets of Asherah who eat at Jezebel's table.”
So Ahab summoned all the Israelites and assembled the prophets on Mount Carmel.

Then Elijah approached all the people and said, “How long will you {{pasach|waver}} between two opinions? If the LORD is God, follow Him. But if Baal is God, follow him.” But the people did not answer a word.
Then Elijah said to the people, “I am the only remaining prophet of the LORD, but Baal has four hundred and fifty prophets.

Get two bulls for us. Let the prophets of Baal choose one bull for themselves, cut it into pieces, and place it on the wood but not light the fire. And I will prepare the other bull and place it on the wood but not light the fire.
Then you may call on the name of your god, and I will call on the name of the LORD. The God who answers by fire, He is God.” And all the people answered, “What you say is good.”

units.label: Baal's prophets cry out in vain
units.note: no voice, no answer, no attention

verses.text: Then Elijah said to the prophets of Baal, “Since you are so numerous, choose for yourselves one bull and prepare it first. Then call on the name of your god, but do not light the fire.”

And they took the bull that was given them, prepared it, and called on the name of Baal from morning until noon, shouting, “O Baal, answer us!” But there was no sound, and no one answered as they leaped around the altar they had made.
At noon Elijah began to taunt them, saying, “Shout louder, for he is a god! Perhaps he is deep in thought, or occupied, or on a journey. Perhaps he is sleeping and must be awakened!”
So they shouted louder and cut themselves with knives and lances, as was their custom, until the blood gushed over them.
Midday passed, and they kept on raving until the time of the evening sacrifice. But there was no response; no one answered, no one paid attention.
Then Elijah said to all the people, “Come near to me.” So all the people approached him, and he repaired the altar of the LORD that had been torn down.
And Elijah took twelve stones, one for each tribe of the sons of Jacob, to whom the word of the LORD had come and said, “Israel shall be your name.”
And with the stones, Elijah built an altar in the name of the LORD. Then he dug a trench around the altar large enough to hold two seahs of seed.
Next, he arranged the wood, cut up the bull, placed it on the wood,

and said, “Fill four waterpots and pour the water on the offering and on the wood.” “Do it a second time,” he said, and they did it a second time. “Do it a third time,” he said, and they did it a third time.
So the water ran down around the altar and even filled the trench.

At the time of the evening sacrifice, Elijah the prophet approached the altar and said, “O LORD, God of Abraham, Isaac, and Israel, let it be known this day that You are God in Israel and that I am Your servant and have done all these things at Your command.
Answer me, O LORD! Answer me, so that this people will know that You, the LORD, are God, and that You have turned their {{lev|hearts}} back again.”
Then the {{esh|fire}} of the LORD fell and consumed the sacrifice, the wood, the stones, and the dust, and it licked up the water in the trench.
When all the people saw this, they fell facedown and said, “The LORD, He is God! The LORD, He is God!”
Then Elijah ordered them, “Seize the prophets of Baal! Do not let a single one escape.” So they seized them, and Elijah brought them down to the Kishon Valley and slaughtered them there.

units.label: The LORD sends rain
units.note: a cloud as small as a man's hand

verses.text: And Elijah said to Ahab, “Go up, eat and drink, for there is the sound of a heavy rain.”
So Ahab went up to eat and drink. But Elijah climbed to the summit of Carmel, bent down on the ground, and put his face between his knees.
“Go and look toward the sea,” he said to his servant. So the servant went and looked, and he said, “There is nothing there.” Seven times Elijah said, “Go back.”

On the seventh time the servant reported, “There is a cloud as small as a man's hand rising from the sea.” And Elijah replied, “Go and tell Ahab, ‘Prepare your chariot and go down before the rain stops you.’”
Meanwhile, the sky grew dark with clouds and wind, and a heavy rain began to fall. So Ahab rode away and went to Jezreel.
And the hand of the LORD came upon Elijah, and he tucked his cloak into his belt and ran ahead of Ahab all the way to Jezreel.

esh.gloss: Fire. The contest's very terms are set by Elijah, the God who answers by esh, He is God, v24. When the LORD's fire finally falls in verse 38, it consumes not only the sacrifice but the wood, the stones, and even the water in the trench, an answer no natural flame could counterfeit and no rival god could match.

ruach.gloss: Spirit, wind, breath. Obadiah fears that once he leaves Elijah, the ruach of the LORD might carry the prophet away to some unknown place, showing how mysterious and untraceable Elijah's movements were already understood to be, guided by the same Spirit who will later empower and direct him.

lev.gloss: Heart, the inner seat of will, loyalty, and belief. Elijah's climactic prayer asks not merely for a spectacle but that the LORD would turn Israel's lev back to Himself, verse 37; the fire that falls serves a far deeper aim than proving a point, the wholehearted return of God's people.

pasach.gloss: To limp, hop, or waver, the same root pictured later in verse 26 when the prophets of Baal literally leap and hobble around their altar. Elijah's charge in verse 21, rendered 'waver,' pictures Israel's divided loyalty as a limping walk, unable to commit fully to either god.

baal.gloss: Baal, meaning lord or master, the storm and fertility god Ahab had enthroned in Samaria, chapter 16. The entire contest on Carmel exists to settle, publicly and decisively, whether the LORD or Baal is truly master over rain, fire, and the hearts of His people.

basic.context: Three years into the drought Elijah announced, the LORD sends him back to Ahab with a promise: rain is coming. On the way Elijah meets Obadiah, a palace official who has secretly protected a hundred prophets of the LORD from Jezebel, and who fears Ahab's fury if Elijah's message proves false. Elijah then confronts Ahab directly and calls for a public contest on Mount Carmel between himself and the prophets of Baal and Asherah. After the prophets of Baal cry out for hours in vain, Elijah rebuilds the LORD's altar, soaks it with water, and prays once, and fire falls from heaven, consuming everything. The people confess the LORD is God, the false prophets are seized, and, after Elijah's persistent prayer, rain finally returns. The chapter raises the question of why Israel had drifted so far, and what it looks like for a whole nation's heart to turn back to God.
basic.hermeneutics: This is historical narrative reporting a specific, unrepeatable public contest and its violent aftermath; it is not a pattern for settling religious disagreements today, nor a general method of evangelism. What endures as timeless truth is that the LORD alone is God, that He answers persevering prayer, and that turning a heart back to Him, even a whole nation's heart, is finally His own gracious work.
basic.originalLanguages: Elijah charges Israel for wavering, pasach, between two opinions, the same word used later of the prophets of Baal limping around their futile altar. His prayer asks that the LORD would turn the people's lev, heart, back to Himself, and the LORD answers with esh, fire, that leaves no doubt. Behind it all is the contest over baal, Baal, Israel's rival god, whose four hundred and fifty prophets cannot produce so much as a voice in reply.
basic.typology: Elijah stands alone against hundreds of false prophets, refusing every compromise, and is vindicated publicly by the LORD's fire. This anticipates a greater Prophet who stood alone before the religious powers of His day and was vindicated not by fire but by resurrection.
basic.christ: Elijah's lone stand for the LORD's exclusive worship, and his vindication by fire before the whole nation, anticipates Christ, who stood alone against the religious establishment and was vindicated by rising from the dead, so that one day every knee will bow and confess Jesus Christ is Lord. Elijah's urgent prayer, answer me, is the pattern James commends, and it points to the far greater confidence believers have in Christ, whose prayers for His people are always heard.
basic.god: God is sovereign over nature, withholding and sending both fire and rain at His word. He is jealous for exclusive worship, unwilling to share His people's hearts with a rival, and He answers persevering, specific prayer. He is the God who turns hearts back to Himself, showing that even Israel's repentance here is finally His own gracious work.
basic.crossReferences: First Kings 17:1 is the promise of rain now finally fulfilled here. James 5:17-18 holds up Elijah's prayer as the pattern of a righteous man's fervent prayer. Romans 11 recalls Elijah's era to teach that God always keeps a remnant for Himself. And Philippians 2:9-11 anticipates the universal confession owed to Christ, of which Israel's cry that the LORD is God is a small foreshadowing.
basic.oneStory: The LORD who shut up the heavens now answers publicly for it, settling for a generation whom Israel must serve and showing His mercy in the rain that follows judgment. It is one more chapter in the long contest for God's people's hearts, resolved fully only in Christ, the true and greater Prophet, before whom every tongue will one day confess that He alone is Lord.

context.literary: The chapter builds through five movements, from private confrontation, Obadiah's fear, to public confrontation, Ahab's accusation, to a carefully staged contest, to the prophets of Baal's escalating, futile desperation, to the LORD's overwhelming answer by fire, and finally to rain granted through Elijah's own persistent, repeated prayer. The narrator lingers over the mockery and self-mutilation of Baal's prophets before compressing the LORD's answer into a single overwhelming verse, 38.
context.historical: Mount Carmel was a prominent ridge on Israel's coast, a natural gathering place visible for miles and historically associated with Baal worship; staging the contest there directly challenged Baal on ground his devotees might have considered his own. The self-laceration of Baal's prophets, verse 28, reflects known Canaanite ritual practice, and the description of Ahab's search for grass for his royal animals, verses 5 to 6, reflects the severity of a three year drought in an agrarian economy.
context.canonical: This chapter is the hinge of the entire Elijah cycle, resolving the drought announced in 17:1 and the idolatry installed in chapter 16, while setting up Elijah's despair and the still small voice of chapter 19. Paul later draws on this very era in Romans 11 to teach that God always preserves a faithful remnant, a truth already visible in Obadiah's hidden prophets here.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show that the LORD alone controls fire and rain, that He publicly vindicates His faithful prophet against overwhelming odds, and that turning Israel's hearts back to exclusive worship of Him is the true aim of the whole contest, not merely a spectacular display of power.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes a specific, unrepeatable public contest and the judicial execution of false prophets under Israel's covenant law; it does not prescribe violence against religious rivals as a pattern for believers today. What endures is the seriousness of idolatry, the reality that the LORD answers persevering prayer, and His sovereign work of turning hearts back to Himself.

debates.question: Was Elijah's slaughter of the prophets of Baal, v40, a model for how believers should treat false teachers today?

views.label: A unique act of covenant justice under Israel's theocracy
views.case: Under the Mosaic covenant, false prophets who led Israel into idolatry were subject to the death penalty, Deuteronomy 13:1-5; Elijah, as the LORD's prophet in a covenant nation whose own king should have executed this law, carries out judicial justice appropriate to that specific theocratic setting.

views.label: A demonstration of God's zeal against idolatry, not a transferable pattern
views.case: The passage highlights the seriousness of leading God's people astray and God's holy jealousy for exclusive worship, but the New Testament redirects such final judgment to the last day and calls the church to spiritual weapons rather than the sword, 2 Corinthians 10:4, Ephesians 6:12.

debates.bearing: Either reading affirms that idolatry is never a light matter to the LORD, and while the church today does not execute rivals to the faith, it must contend for the exclusive worship and lordship of Christ with equal, if differently expressed, seriousness.

debates.question: What does Elijah's taunting of the prophets of Baal, v27, teach about how to engage false worship?

views.label: A legitimate rhetorical tool exposing the emptiness of idols
views.case: Scripture elsewhere mocks idols directly, Isaiah 44:9-20, Psalm 115:4-8; Elijah's sarcasm serves to expose publicly, before all Israel, that Baal is nothing, a prophetic unmasking of a false god's impotence rather than a personal vendetta.

views.label: A situational tactic tied to this specific, decisive contest
views.case: The mockery belongs to the unique drama of this one time, public test of whose god can answer; it is not offered elsewhere in Scripture as a general method for evangelism or apologetics, which more often calls for gentleness and respect, 1 Peter 3:15.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree the point is to expose Baal's utter powerlessness against the living God who truly hears and answers, not to commend mockery as an ordinary posture toward unbelievers.

study.originalLanguages: Elijah charges the wavering assembly with pasach, a word that pictures limping or hopping between two positions, the very same root used later of the prophets of Baal literally leaping and hobbling around their altar in their desperation. The contest turns entirely on esh, fire, the sign both sides agree will settle whose god truly is God, and when the LORD's esh finally falls it consumes stones and water alike, leaving nothing to explain away. Behind it all stands baal, Baal, the rival lord Ahab has enthroned, and Elijah's climactic prayer asks the LORD to do something deeper than send fire, to turn the lev, the heart, of His people back to Himself.

typology.type: The true prophet who calls fire down from heaven to vindicate the LORD before a wavering people
typology.fulfillment: Christ, of whom even His enemies could find no charge, and who will one day be revealed with fire, 2 Thessalonians 1:7-8, having already proven Himself the true and greater Prophet, John 6:14.

typology.type: One faithful voice standing against hundreds of false prophets at great personal risk
typology.fulfillment: Christ standing alone against the religious establishment of His day, and every faithful witness who stands for the truth though outnumbered, Romans 11:2-5, Hebrews 12:1-3.

typology.type: Water poured out in abundance before the fire falls, removing any doubt of a natural explanation
typology.fulfillment: The Spirit poured out at Pentecost removing all doubt that the church's boldness and unity is a work of God rather than human effort, Acts 2:1-4.

study.christ: Elijah stands alone as the LORD's prophet against four hundred and fifty prophets of Baal, refusing to compromise or share the LORD's worship with a rival, and the LORD vindicates him publicly by fire so that all Israel confesses, the LORD, He is God. Centuries later, a greater than Elijah stood alone before the religious powers of His day, refusing every compromise, and was vindicated not by fire consuming an altar but by rising from the dead, so that one day every knee will bow and confess Jesus Christ is Lord, Philippians 2:10-11. Elijah's urgent, believing prayer, answer me, O LORD, answer me, is the very pattern James commends, and it points to the far greater confidence believers now have in Christ, our great High Priest, whose prayers for His people are always heard, John 11:41-42, Hebrews 7:25.
study.god: God is sovereign over nature, withholding and sending both fire and rain at His word. He is jealous for exclusive worship, unwilling to share His people's hearts with Baal, and this jealousy is itself covenant love, not petty rivalry. He answers persevering, specific prayer, both Elijah's prayer for fire and his later, patient prayer for rain, and He is the God who turns hearts back to Himself, showing that even genuine repentance, verses 37 and 39, is finally His own gracious work.

crossReferences.note: Elijah's original oath that there would be no rain except at his word is the promise now finally fulfilled in this chapter's closing verses.
The law's provision for dealing with prophets who lead Israel into idolatry stands behind Elijah's action against the prophets of Baal.
James holds up Elijah as a man with a nature like ours whose fervent, righteous prayer shut and opened the heavens, directly referencing this chapter's drought and rain.
Paul recalls Elijah's despair in the next chapter to teach that God always keeps a remnant for Himself, a truth already on display in Obadiah's hidden prophets here, verses 3 to 4.
The universal confession Israel makes on Carmel, that the LORD alone is God, anticipates the universal confession owed to Christ at His name.

study.oneStory: The LORD who shut up the heavens now sends His prophet to answer for it publicly, on a mountain long associated with Baal worship, before the very king and queen who installed that idolatry in Israel. The fire that falls settles, for that generation, whom Israel must serve, and the rain that follows shows His mercy is not only justice but restoration. It is one more chapter in the long contest for the hearts of God's people, resolved only fully when the true and greater Prophet, Jesus Christ, both answers for His people and is Himself the Judge and the Redeemer, before whom every tongue will one day confess that He alone is Lord.

sermon.title: God Rules the Heart of Every Man: Elijah's Battle with the Prophets of Baal
sermon.why: Preached at the London Men's Convention, Piper works through 1 Kings 18:16-46 verse by verse, drawing out the climax in verse 37 that the LORD turns hearts back to Himself, closely matching this movement's full sweep from confrontation to fire to rain.

quiz.q: Why did Obadiah initially resist Elijah's instruction to tell Ahab that Elijah was present, vv 9-14?

options.text: He feared that once he left, Elijah might vanish and Ahab would kill him for a false report, especially since Ahab had already searched every nation for Elijah in vain.
options.note: This is exactly the fear Obadiah voices across verses 9 to 14.

options.text: He personally disagreed with Elijah's message and confrontation of Ahab.
options.note: The text shows fear for his own life, not disagreement with Elijah's message.

options.text: He wanted Ahab to remain unaware of Elijah's whereabouts forever.
options.note: Obadiah does go and inform Ahab, verse 16, so this misstates his actual response.

options.text: He refused outright and never told Ahab anything.
options.note: The text explicitly says Obadiah went to inform Ahab, verse 16.

quiz.q: What accusation does Ahab level at Elijah, and how does Elijah respond, vv 17-18?

options.text: Ahab calls Elijah 'troubler of Israel'; Elijah answers that Ahab and his father's house have troubled Israel by forsaking the LORD's commandments and following the Baals.
options.note: This is the exact exchange recorded in verses 17 and 18.

options.text: Ahab accuses Elijah of causing the famine, and Elijah apologizes for it.
options.note: Elijah does not apologize; he turns the accusation back onto Ahab's own idolatry.

options.text: Ahab praises Elijah for ending the drought early.
options.note: The drought has not yet ended at this point in the narrative; this contradicts the scene.

options.text: Ahab welcomes Elijah warmly with no accusation at all.
options.note: The opposite occurs; Ahab confronts Elijah as a troubler of Israel.

quiz.q: What were the terms of the contest Elijah proposed on Mount Carmel, vv 23-24?

options.text: Two bulls would be prepared and laid on wood without fire lit; each side would call on their god, and the God who answered by fire would be recognized as the true God.
options.note: These are the exact terms Elijah lays out in verses 23 and 24.

options.text: Whoever could pray the longest without stopping would be declared the true prophet.
options.note: The stated terms concern fire falling on a sacrifice, not endurance in prayer.

options.text: The people themselves would simply vote on which god they preferred.
options.note: The resolution comes from God's fire, not a popular vote.

options.text: The contest involved a footrace between Elijah and the prophets of Baal.
options.note: This is unrelated to the actual terms described in the text.

quiz.q: How did the prophets of Baal behave when their god did not answer, vv 26-29?

options.text: They leaped around their altar, shouted louder, and cut themselves with knives and lances until they bled, yet there was no voice, no answer, and no response.
options.note: This matches the escalating, futile behavior described across verses 26 to 29.

options.text: They quietly admitted defeat and left the mountain.
options.note: The text describes frantic, escalating behavior, not a quiet withdrawal.

options.text: They successfully lit their own fire after much effort.
options.note: No fire ever comes for them; the text states there was no response at all.

options.text: Baal answered them clearly, but Elijah refused to acknowledge it.
options.note: The text states plainly there was no voice and no answer of any kind.

quiz.q: What did Elijah do to the altar of the LORD, and why did he pour water on it repeatedly, vv 30-35?

options.text: He repaired the torn down altar with twelve stones representing the tribes of Israel, then drenched the sacrifice and wood with water three times so no natural explanation for the coming fire would be possible.
options.note: This matches the deliberate details of verses 30 to 35.

options.text: He built a brand new altar unrelated to Israel's twelve tribes.
options.note: The twelve stones deliberately represent the tribes of Israel, verse 31.

options.text: He poured water on the altar to cool the stones before lighting a fire himself.
options.note: Elijah never lights a fire himself; the LORD alone sends the fire.

options.text: He left the altar exactly as it was found, torn down.
options.note: The text says he actively repaired the altar, verse 30.

quiz.q: What happened immediately after the LORD's fire fell, and how did the chapter end, vv 39-46?

options.text: All the people fell facedown, confessing 'The LORD, He is God'; the prophets of Baal were seized and slaughtered, and after Elijah's persistent prayer for rain, a great rain came and Elijah ran ahead of Ahab's chariot to Jezreel.
options.note: This summarizes the sequence of events across verses 39 to 46.

options.text: The people scattered in fear and never professed faith in the LORD.
options.note: The text records their explicit confession, not scattering in silence.

options.text: Ahab immediately turned from Baal and destroyed Jezebel's altars himself.
options.note: No such change in Ahab is narrated in this chapter.

options.text: No rain ever came despite Elijah's prayer, leaving the drought unresolved.
options.note: The text describes a great rain finally falling, verses 44 to 45.

questions: Elijah confronted Ahab directly, naming his sin plainly rather than softening it. Where do you need the courage to speak an uncomfortable truth plainly instead of avoiding conflict?
How long will you waver between two opinions, Elijah asked a divided nation. Where in your own life are you still trying to serve two masters rather than committing fully to the LORD?
The prophets of Baal wore themselves out crying to a god who could not answer. What are you currently pouring your energy and hope into that simply cannot respond or deliver?

Elijah deliberately made the LORD's task harder, soaking the offering in water, so no one could doubt the miracle was God's alone. Where might you be relying on your own resources instead of trusting God for something only He can do?

Elijah prayed once for fire and it fell instantly, but he prayed seven times, patiently, before the rain cloud finally appeared. Where do you need to keep praying persistently rather than giving up after one attempt?
The whole nation cried, the LORD, He is God, yet within a chapter Elijah himself despairs. How do you guard your own heart against discouragement right after a season of great spiritual victory?

translationNotes.note: The BSB footnote renders the opening literally as 'And the days were many, and in the third year,' marking the long, grinding length of the drought before this chapter's resolution.

The Hebrew verb behind 'waver' pictures a limping or hopping gait, the same word used of the prophets of Baal 'leaping' around their altar in verse 26, linking Elijah's charge and their futile ritual in the original language.
The BSB footnote gives two seahs as roughly 13.2 dry quarts or 14.6 liters, indicating a substantial trench dug to hold the water poured over the offering.
The BSB footnote explains that 'he tucked his cloak into his belt' renders the Hebrew idiom 'he girded up his loins,' a practical preparation for running.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for 1 Kings 18:1-46. No em dashes or en dashes appear in the source text for this chapter.
